prompts.baseline:
You are an OCR system. Transcribe ALL text from this image exactly as written.

Rules:
- Output ONLY the transcribed text, nothing else
- Preserve line breaks and paragraph structure
- Read every line from top to bottom
- Do not add any commentary, descriptions, or explanations
- Do not say "Here is the text" or similar - just output the raw text

Begin transcription:
prompts.simple: Transcribe all text from this image exactly as written. Output only the text, nothing else.
prompts.accuracy:
OCR this document with maximum accuracy.
- Read every character carefully
- If unsure about a character, use your best judgment based on context
- Preserve all formatting including indentation and spacing
- Output only the transcribed text
prompts.clean:
Transcribe this document precisely.
- Output clean text only - no asterisks, symbols, or placeholders for unclear characters
- If a character is unclear, infer the most likely character from context
- Preserve paragraph breaks and line structure
- Do not add any markers or annotations
prompts.formatting:
Transcribe this typewritten document exactly.
- Preserve all indentation (tabs and spaces)
- Keep original line breaks
- Maintain paragraph spacing
- Copy punctuation exactly as shown
- Output only the transcribed text, no commentary
prompts.expert:
You are an expert OCR system trained on typewritten documents from the 1940s-1960s.
Transcribe this document with perfect accuracy:
- Recognize common typewriter fonts and spacing
- Handle faded or slightly unclear characters using context
- Preserve the original document structure exactly
- Output clean text without any markup or annotations
prompts.structured:
Extract all text from this image.
Requirements:
1. Transcribe every word exactly as written
2. Preserve line breaks with newlines
3. Preserve paragraph breaks with double newlines
4. Keep indentation using spaces
5. No headers, labels, or commentary - just the raw text
prompts.careful:
Carefully transcribe this document character by character.
Common OCR errors to avoid:
- Don't confuse 'l' and '1' or 'O' and '0'
- Watch for faded characters - use context to determine correct letter
- Don't insert placeholder symbols (*, x, etc.) for unclear text
- Preserve all whitespace and formatting
Output only the final transcribed text.
prompts.context:
This is a typewritten document. Transcribe it exactly.
The document may contain:
- Letters, memos, or notes
- Lists with indentation
- Dates and numbers
- Proper names
Read carefully and output only the text content, preserving all formatting.
prompts.fewshot-clean:
You are an OCR system. Here's an example of correct transcription:

EXAMPLE INPUT: [Image of faded typewritten text reading "The qui_k brown f_x"]
CORRECT OUTPUT: The quick brown fox

Note: Unclear characters were inferred from context - no asterisks or placeholders.

Now transcribe the following image exactly as written. Output only the text.
prompts.fewshot-outline:
You are an OCR system. Here's an example of correct outline transcription:

EXAMPLE:
I.   Main Topic
     A. First subtopic
     B. Second subtopic
        1. Detail one
        2. Detail two

II.  Next Topic
     A. Subtopic here

Note the formatting: Roman numerals aligned, letters indented, numbers further indented.

Now transcribe the document image exactly as written, preserving this outline structure.
prompts.fewshot-noartifact:
Transcribe this typewritten document.

IMPORTANT - Never output:
- Asterisks (*) for unclear characters
- Question marks (?) for uncertain letters
- Any placeholder symbols

Instead, when text is unclear, infer the correct character from context.

Example: If you see "photogr_phy", output "photography" (not "photogr*phy").

Output only the clean transcribed text:
prompts.fewshot-complete:
You are an expert OCR system. Follow these examples:

HANDLING UNCLEAR TEXT:
- "daguerr_otype" → "daguerreotype" (infer from word context)
- "tables_nd chairs" → "tables and chairs" (infer missing space)

PRESERVING FORMAT:
I.   Topic Name
     A. Subtopic
        1. Detail

Rules:
- Never use *, ?, or placeholders for unclear text
- Preserve indentation with spaces
- Keep original line breaks

Transcribe the image now:

details.actual:
THE CLOCK ON THE MANTLE

The house had been empty for eleven years when
Sarah finally returned. She stood in the doorway, her
suitcase at her feet, and listened,

Silence. Complete and heavy.

No -- not quite. From somewhere deeper in the
house came a sound. Tick. Tick. Tick. The old
grandfather clock in the parlor, stillkeeping time
after all these years. Who had been winding it?

She stepped inside. The floorboards creaked
beneath her feet, announcing her presence to no one
in particular. Dust covered everything -- the tables,
the chairs, the photographs on the walls. Faces she
barely recognized anymore.

The parlor door was closed.

"Hello?" she called out, feeling foolish.

Tick. Tick. Tick.

She reached for the handle--

continued on page 2
details.expected:

	The house had been empty for eleven years when
Sarah finally returned. She stood in the doorway,
her suitcase at her feet, and listened.

	Silence. Complete and heavy.

	No -- not quite. From somewhere deeper in the
house came a sound. Tick. Tick. Tick. The old
grandfather clock in the parlor, still keeping time
after all these years. Who had been winding it?

	She stepped inside. The floorboards creaked
beneath her feet, announcing her presence to no one
in particular. Dust covered everything -- the tables,
the chairs, the photographs on the walls. Faces she
barely recognized anymore.

	The parlor door was closed.

	"Hello?" she called out, feeling foolish.

	Tick. Tick. Tick.

	She reached for the handle--

				(continued on page 2)

details.actual:
THE MORNING TRAIN

The platform empty, still, and grey,
Before the crowd arrives --
I stand alone and watch the rails
Stretch out like silver knives.

A distant whistle, low and long,
Cuts through the morning air.
The engine rounds the final bend,
All steam and smoke and glare.

The doors slide open, people rush,
A hundred lives collide.
And I step on, and find my seat,
And watch the world outside.

* * *

The city shrinks behind the glass,
The fields roll into view.
And somewhere in this rocking car,
I start my life anew.

-- Anonymous, 1947

	The city shrinks behind the glass,

	And somewhere in this rocking car,
	I start my life anew.

				-- Anonymous, 1947

details.actual:
A BRIEF HISTORY OF PHOTOGRAPHY  
Lecture Outline – Prof. Williams

I. Early Experiments 1820-1840  
A. Camera obscura principles  
B. Niepce and the first photograph  
C. Daguerre's contributions  
  1. The daguerreotype process  
  2. Commercial applications  

II. The Wet Plate Era 1851-1880  
A. Collodion process  
B. Advantages over daguerreotype  
  - Shorter exposure times  
  - Reproducible negatives  
C. Notable practitioners  
  1. Mathew Brady  
  2. Julia Margaret Cameron  

III. The Kodak Revolution 1888-1920  
A. "You press the button, we do the rest"  
B. Roll film development  
C. Amateur photography emerges  

IV. Questions for Discussion  
- How did photography change portraiture?  
- What social changes did it enable?
details.expected:
A BRIEF HISTORY OF PHOTOGRAPHY
Lecture Outline - Prof. Williams

I.   Early Experiments (1820-1840)
     A. Camera obscura principles
     B. Niepce and the first photograph
     C. Daguerre's contributions
	  1. The daguerreotype process
	  2. Commercial applications

II.  The Wet Plate Era (1851-1880)
     A. Collodion process
     B. Advantages over daguerreotype
	  - Shorter exposure times
	  - Reproducible negatives
     C. Notable practitioners
	  1. Mathew Brady
	  2. Julia Margaret Cameron

III. The Kodak Revolution (1888-1920)
     A. "You press the button, we do the rest"
     B. Roll film development
     C. Amateur photography emerges

IV.  Questions for Discussion
     - How did photography change portraiture?
     - What social changes did it enable?

details.actual:
PLANNING COMMITTEE MEETING
November 3, 1958
----------------------------

Present: J. Harrison, M. Webb, R. Collins, S. Park

1. Budget Review
- Current balance: $4,250.00
- Outstanding invoices: $890.00
- Projected surplus: $1,100.00

2. Spring Festival Planning
a) Date confirmed: May 15-17
b) Venue: Memorial Park (pending approval)
c) Entertainment committee to report by Dec. 1

3. Action Items
Harrison -- Contact city office re: permits
Webb -- Draft volunteer schedule
Collins -- Get quotes from three caterers
Park -- Reserve tables and chairs

Next meeting: November 17, 2:00 PM

details.actual:
October 15, 1962

Dear Margaret,

I hope this letter finds you well. It has been far too long since we last spoke, and I find myself thinking of our conversations often.

The autumn here has been particularly beautiful this year. The leaves along the drive have turned a brilliant shade of orange, and the garden is still producing tomatoes despite the cooler nights.

I wanted to ask about the book you mentioned—the one about the lighthouse keeper. I have been unable to find it at our local shop, and wondered if you might send me the author's name.

With warm regards,  
Eleanor

P.S. - Do give my best to your mother.
details.expected:
October 15, 1962

Dear Margaret,

	I hope this letter finds you well. It has been
far too long since we last spoke, and I find myself
thinking of our conversations often.

	The autumn here has been particularly beautiful
this year. The maples along the drive have turned
a brilliant shade of orange, and the garden is
still producing tomatoes despite the cooler nights.

	I wanted to ask about the book you mentioned --
the one about the lighthouse keeper. I have been
unable to find it at our local shop, and wondered
if you might send me the author's name.

		With warm regards,
		Eleanor

P.S. - Do give my best to your mother.

details.actual:
March 12

Woke early today -- before six -- and could not get back to sleep. Made coffee and watched the sun come up over the rooftops.

The plumber finally came about the kitchen sink. Three weeks of waiting and it took him twenty minutes to fix. The bill: $45.00. Highway robbery, but what can you do?

Afternoon: walked to the library. Returned the Hemingway and picked up two new ones --

"The Old Man and the Sea" (Hemingway again)
"East of Eden" by Steinbeck

Started the Steinbeck after dinner. Very good so far.

Note to self:
- Call Mother on Sunday
- Pick up dry cleaning (the blue suit)
- Pay electric bill before the 20th

To bed early. Rain expected tomorrow.

	"East of Eden" by Steinbeck

Started the Steinbeck after dinner. Very good so far.

	- Call Mother on Sunday
	- Pick up dry cleaning (the blue suit)
	- Pay electric bill before the 20th

	To bed early. Rain expected tomorrow.

details.actual:
HOW TO DEVELOP BLACK AND WHITE FILM
====================================

MATERIALS NEEDED:
- Developing tank and reel
- Developer (0-76 recommended)
- Stop bath
- Fixer
- Thermometer
- Timer
- Film clips
- Dark bag or darkroom

PROCEDURE:
Step 1 -- Load the film
In complete darkness, open the film canister and wind the film onto the reel. Place in tank and secure lid.

Step 2 -- Mix chemicals
Developer: 68 degrees F (20 C)
Stop bath: 65-70 degrees F
Fixer: 65-70 degrees F

Step 3 -- Develop
Pour developer into tank. Start timer. 
Agitate for first 30 seconds, then 5 seconds every 30 seconds. Total time: 7-9 minutes (check film data sheet).

Step 4 -- Stop and fix
Pour out developer. Add stop bath for 30 sec. 
Pour out. Add fixer for 5 minutes.

Step 5 -- Wash and dry
Running water wash: 10 minutes minimum. 
Hang to dry in dust-free area.

CAUTION: Chemicals can stain. Work carefully.

HOW TO DEVELOP BLACK AND WHITE FILM
====================================

  - Developing tank and reel
  - Developer (D-76 recommended)
  - Stop bath
  - Fixer
  - Thermometer

  - Film clips

	In complete darkness, open the film canister
	and wind the film onto the reel. Place in
	tank and secure lid.

Step 2 -- Mix chemicals
	Developer:  68 degrees F (20 C)
	Stop bath:  65-70 degrees F
	Fixer:      65-70 degrees F

Step 3 -- Develop
	Pour developer into tank. Start timer.
	Agitate for first 30 seconds, then 5 seconds
	every 30 seconds. Total time: 7-9 minutes
	(check film data sheet).

Step 4 -- Stop and fix
	Pour out developer. Add stop bath for 30 sec.
	Pour out. Add fixer for 5 minutes.

Step 5 -- Wash and dry
	Running water wash: 10 minutes minimum.
	Hang to dry in dust-free area.

CAUTION: Chemicals can stain. Work carefully.
